title: The Man From Waterloo
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was the Man from Waterloo,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When work in town was slack,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who took the track as bushmen do,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And humped his swag out back.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He tramped for months without a bob,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For most the sheds were full,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until at last he got a job
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At picking up the wool.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He found the work was rather rough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But swore to see it through,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he was made of sterling stuff,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Man from Waterloo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The first remark was like a stab
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That fell his ear upon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas, ן¿½Thereן¿½s another something scab
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The boss has taken on!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They couldnן¿½t let the towny be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They sneered like anything;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½d mock him when heן¿½d sound the ן¿½gן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In words that end in ן¿½ing.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There came a man from Ironbark,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And at the shed he shore;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He scoffed his victuals like a shark,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And like a fiend he swore.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½d shorn his flowing beard that day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He found it hard to reap,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Because ן¿½twas hot and in the way
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While he was shearing sheep.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His loaded fork in grimy holt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was poised, his jaws moved fast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Impatient till his throat could bolt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mouthful taken last.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He couldnן¿½t stand a something toff;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Much less a jackaroo;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And swore to take the trimmings off
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Man from Waterloo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The towny saw he must be up
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or else be underneath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so one day, before them all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He dared to clean his teeth.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The men came running from the shed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And shouted, ן¿½Hereן¿½s a lark!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s gone to clean its tooties!ן¿½ said
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The man from Ironbark.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His feeble joke was much enjoyed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He sneered as bullies do,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with a scrubbing-brush he guyed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Man from Waterloo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Jackaroo made no remark
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But peeled and waded in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And soon the Man from Ironbark
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had three teeth less to grin!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when they knew that he could fight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They swore to see him through,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Because they saw that he was right,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Man from Waterloo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now in a shop in Sydney, near
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Bottle on the Shelf,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The tale is told, with trimmings, by
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Jackaroo himself.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They made my life a hell,ן¿½ he said;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They wouldnן¿½t let me be;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They set the bully of the shed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To take it out of me.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The dirt was on him like a sheath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He seldom washed his phiz;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He sneered because I cleaned my teeth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I guess I dusted his!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I treated them as they deserved,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I signed on one or two!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½They wonן¿½t forget me soon,ן¿½ observed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Man from Waterloo.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
